citation.section: Playing the Game > Damage and Healing
citation.quote: Damage reduces a creature's current Hit Points. When a creature takes damage, subtract it from the creature's current Hit Points.

citation.section: Playing the Game > Damage and Healing > Falling Unconscious
citation.quote: If you reach 0 Hit Points and don't die instantly, you have the Unconscious condition until you regain any Hit Points.

citation.section: Playing the Game > Damage and Healing > Healing
citation.quote: A creature's current Hit Points can't exceed its Hit Point maximum, so any Hit Points regained in excess of this number are lost.

citation.section: Playing the Game > Damage and Healing > Instant Death > Massive Damage
citation.quote: When damage reduces a character to 0 Hit Points and damage remains, the character dies if the remainder equals or exceeds their Hit Point maximum.

citation.section: Playing the Game > Damage and Healing > Instant Death > Monster Death
citation.quote: A monster dies the instant it drops to 0 Hit Points, although a Game Master can ignore this rule for an individual monster.

citation.section: Playing the Game > Damage and Healing > Death Saving Throws
citation.quote: Roll 1d20. If the roll is 10 or higher, you succeed. Otherwise, you fail. On your third success, you become Stable. On your third failure, you die.

citation.section: Playing the Game > Damage and Healing > Death Saving Throws > Rolling a 1 or 20
citation.quote: When you roll a 1 on the d20 for a Death Saving Throw, you suffer two failures. If you roll a 20 on the d20, you regain 1 Hit Point.

citation.section: Playing the Game > Damage and Healing > Death Saving Throws
citation.quote: The number of both is reset to zero when you regain any Hit Points or become Stable.

citation.section: Playing the Game > Damage and Healing > Death Saving Throws > Damage at 0 Hit Points
citation.quote: If you take any damage while you have 0 Hit Points, you suffer a Death Saving Throw failure. If the damage is from a Critical Hit, you suffer two failures instead. If the damage equals or exceeds your Hit Point maximum, you die.
If the total of the d20 and its modifiers equals or exceeds the target number, the D20 Test succeeds. Otherwise, it fails.
The DC equals 10 or half the damage taken (round down), whichever number is higher, up to a maximum DC of 30.

citation.section: Playing the Game > Damage and Healing > Resistance and Vulnerability
citation.quote: If you have Resistance to a damage type, damage of that type is halved against you (round down).

citation.section: Playing the Game > Damage and Healing > Resistance and Vulnerability
citation.quote: If you have Vulnerability to a damage type, damage of that type is doubled against you.

citation.section: Playing the Game > Damage and Healing > Immunity
citation.quote: Immunity to a damage type means you don't take damage of that type.

citation.section: Playing the Game > Damage and Healing > Order of Application
citation.quote: Modifiers to damage are applied in the following order: adjustments such as bonuses, penalties, or multipliers are applied first; Resistance is applied second; and Vulnerability is applied third.
